Abhorrent.kidExplanation: From Latin words meaning "to shrink back" — when something is so disgusting you literally step away.

Adamant.kidExplanation: The Greeks used this word for the hardest substance they knew — the same root gave us "diamond".

Audacious.kidExplanation: From a Latin word for "daring". A cousin of "audacity" — boldness that takes nerve.

Belligerent.kidExplanation: Built from Latin "bellum" (war) and "gerere" (to wage). Literally "war-waging" — ready to fight.

Benevolent.kidExplanation: Latin "bene" (well) + "volens" (wishing). Someone benevolent wishes you well — the opposite of malevolent.

Cacophony.kidExplanation: Greek "kakos" (bad) + "phone" (sound). The opposite of symphony — noise that hurts your ears.

Calamity.kidExplanation: Originally a Roman word for a crop disaster — when the harvest is ruined, that's a calamity.

Captivating.kidExplanation: Same Latin root as "capture" — something so interesting it seizes your attention.

Clandestine.kidExplanation: From Latin "clam" meaning "secretly". A clandestine plan is one nobody is meant to see.

Compassion.kidExplanation: Latin "cum" (with) + "pati" (to suffer). Compassion is literally feeling someone else's pain alongside them.

Courageous.kidExplanation: From Latin "cor" meaning "heart". Courage was thought to live in the heart — being brave is being big-hearted.

Decrepit.kidExplanation: Latin for "creaky and worn out" — used for crumbling old buildings and very ancient things.

Desolate.kidExplanation: Latin "sole" means "alone". A desolate place is one that has been completely abandoned.

Eerie.kidExplanation: An old Scottish word originally meaning "scared". Now we use it for things that make us feel scared.

Exquisite.kidExplanation: Latin for "carefully sought out" — something so fine and rare you had to search hard to find it.

Ferocious.kidExplanation: From Latin "ferox" meaning fierce and wild — the same family as "feral" (gone wild).

Forlorn.kidExplanation: An old English word literally meaning "completely lost" — used now for sad and hopeless feelings.

Frantic.kidExplanation: From a Greek root for "the mind" — a frantic person's mind is racing out of control.

Idyllic.kidExplanation: Greek for "a tiny picture" — first used for short poems about peaceful country life, like a postcard scene.

Labyrinthine.kidExplanation: From the Greek myth of the Labyrinth — the impossible maze built by Daedalus to hold the Minotaur.

Lethargic.kidExplanation: Linked to Lethe, the river of forgetfulness in Greek myth. Drink from it and you'd feel sleepy and slow.

Magnanimous.kidExplanation: Latin "magnus" (great) + "animus" (soul). Literally "big-souled" — generous-hearted, above small grudges.

Majestic.kidExplanation: Same root as "majesty" — the word kings and queens get. Anything majestic feels royal in scale.

Malevolent.kidExplanation: Latin "male" (badly) + "volens" (wishing). The exact opposite of "benevolent" — wishing someone harm.

Nefarious.kidExplanation: Latin "nefas" meant something so wicked it shouldn't even be spoken. Nefarious plans are deeply, secretly evil.

Pristine.kidExplanation: Originally meant "like it was at the beginning" — perfectly fresh and unspoilt, as if brand new.

Resilient.kidExplanation: Latin "re-" (back) + "salire" (to jump). A resilient person bounces back after every setback.

Stealthy.kidExplanation: Same Old English root as "steal" — a thief's quiet way of moving so nobody notices.

Tempestuous.kidExplanation: Latin "tempestas" first meant "weather", then "storm". A tempestuous mood is a thunderstorm of feelings.

Tumultuous.kidExplanation: Roman crowds shouting and shoving were called "tumultus" — anything tumultuous is wild and noisy.

Abundant.kidExplanation: From Latin 'ab' (from) + 'unda' (wave). Abundant literally means waves spilling over — so much it overflows.

Acrimonious.kidExplanation: From Latin 'acer' meaning sharp or bitter. Same root as 'acrid' smoke that stings your eyes. An acrimonious argument has a very sharp edge.

Adversary.kidExplanation: Latin 'ad' (towards) + 'vertere' (to turn). An adversary is someone who has turned against you — facing you in opposition.

Affable.kidExplanation: From Latin 'ad' (to) + 'fari' (to speak). An affable person is easy to speak to — approachable and warm.

Amiable.kidExplanation: From Latin 'amare' (to love). Same root as 'amigo' (friend) and 'amorous' (loving). Literally: someone easy to love.

Anticipation.kidExplanation: Latin 'ante' (before) + 'capere' (to take). Anticipation is mentally grabbing something before it has happened.

Arduous.kidExplanation: From a Latin word meaning 'steep' — originally used for difficult mountain climbs. Now any hard, exhausting effort is arduous.

Bewildered.kidExplanation: From 'be-' + 'wilder' (to lead into the wilderness). Being bewildered is like being dropped in a wild forest with no map.

Bleak.kidExplanation: From an Old Norse word meaning pale and colourless. A bleak landscape is washed-out and bare — the colour drained from it.

Bliss.kidExplanation: One of the oldest English words for happiness. Shares a root with 'blithe' (carefree). Pure, simple, complete joy.

Capricious.kidExplanation: From Italian 'capro' meaning goat. Goats leap in all directions without warning — a capricious person changes their mind just as suddenly.

Chaotic.kidExplanation: Greek 'khaos' was the formless void that existed before the universe. Chaos gave birth to everything — so anything chaotic is before order is made.

Consensus.kidExplanation: Latin 'con' (together) + 'sentire' (to feel). Reaching consensus means everyone feels the same way — a meeting of minds.

Daunt.kidExplanation: From a French word for taming wild animals. Something daunting tames your courage — it makes a brave person hesitate.

Dazzling.rootMeaning: to be breathless, to be dazed
Dazzling.kidExplanation: From the same root as 'daze' — something so bright or impressive it leaves you momentarily breathless and confused.

Defiant.kidExplanation: From Old French 'defier' meaning to break faith with someone. A defiant person openly refuses to submit or obey.

Delicate.kidExplanation: From a Latin word for something so fine and pleasing it must be handled carefully. Same family as 'delicious' and 'delight'.

Despondent.rootMeaning: to give up entirely, to lose heart
Despondent.kidExplanation: Latin 'de' (completely) + 'spondere' (to pledge). To be despondent is to give up all your hopes — as if breaking a promise to yourself.

Dilapidated.kidExplanation: From Latin 'dis' (apart) + 'lapis' (stone). A dilapidated building has its stones scattered — falling apart stone by stone.

Dwindle.kidExplanation: From an Old English word meaning to waste away like a candle burning down. Something dwindles when it gets smaller and smaller until almost nothing.

Elated.kidExplanation: Latin 'ex' (out) + 'latus' (carried). To be elated is to be lifted up and carried high — your spirits raised off the ground.

Ephemeral.kidExplanation: Greek 'epi' (upon) + 'hemera' (day). Something ephemeral lasts only a single day — like a mayfly that lives just one morning.

Equilibrium.kidExplanation: Latin 'aequus' (equal) + 'libra' (scales). The same 'libra' that names the zodiac sign of the scales — perfect, even balance.

Eradicate.rootMeaning: to tear up by the roots
Eradicate.kidExplanation: Latin 'e' (out) + 'radix' (root). To eradicate is to pull something out completely by its roots so it can never grow back.

Espionage.kidExplanation: From Italian 'spiare' meaning to spy, through French 'espion'. Espionage is the organised system of spying — secret information gathering.

Exacerbate.kidExplanation: Latin 'ex' (intensifier) + 'acer' (sharp, bitter). To exacerbate means to make something sharper — to make a bad situation worse.

Flourish.kidExplanation: From Latin 'flos' meaning flower. To flourish is to bloom like a flower — growing and thriving at your best.

Fragile.kidExplanation: Latin 'frangere' means to break. Fragile, fracture, and fragment all share this root — something fragile is just waiting to fracture.

Gargantuan.kidExplanation: Named after Gargantua, the enormous giant in Rabelais' 1534 novel — a creature so huge he accidentally swallowed pilgrims. His name became the word for truly massive.

Garrulous.kidExplanation: From a Latin word imitating the sound of chattering birds. A garrulous person talks and talks — just like a bird that never stops.

Hierarchy.kidExplanation: Greek 'hieros' (sacred) + 'arkhia' (rule). Originally the ranked order of angels and priests — now any system with levels of power.

Indignant.kidExplanation: Latin 'in' (not) + 'dignus' (worthy). Feeling indignant means feeling that something unworthy or unfair has happened to you — righteous anger.

Ingenious.kidExplanation: Latin 'in' + 'gignere' (to be born with). Cleverness you were born with — the same root that gives us 'genius' and 'engineer'.

Jovial.kidExplanation: From Latin 'Iovis' — the name of Jupiter, king of the gods. People born under Jupiter were thought to be naturally cheerful and merry.

Jubilant.kidExplanation: From Latin 'jubilare' (to shout with joy), originally from a Hebrew shout of celebration called 'Jubilee'. A jubilant crowd is shouting for joy.

Melancholy.kidExplanation: Greek 'melas' (black) + 'khole' (bile). Ancient Greek doctors thought sadness was caused by too much black bile in the body — a medical word that became a feeling.

Menacing.kidExplanation: From Latin 'minari' (to threaten) — originally about cliffs that jut out dangerously. Something menacing hangs over you threateningly.

Mercurial.kidExplanation: Named after Mercury — the Roman god who moved so fast he had wings on his feet. A mercurial person's moods change as quickly as quicksilver.

Mischievous.kidExplanation: From Old French 'mes' (bad) + 'chief' (end, head). A mischievous person is heading towards a bad ending — or pulling others that way.

Nostalgic.kidExplanation: Greek 'nostos' (return home) + 'algos' (pain). A Swiss doctor invented this word in 1688 as a medical condition — extreme homesickness he thought could be fatal.

Notorious.kidExplanation: Latin 'nota' means a mark or sign. 'Notorious' first just meant 'well-known' — but being widely known is usually for something bad, so the word gained its dark edge.

Ominous.kidExplanation: Directly from Latin 'omen' — a sign of things to come. When Romans saw strange birds or lightning, those were omens. Something ominous carries a warning sign.

Opulent.kidExplanation: From Latin 'opes' meaning wealth and resources. Opulent means dripping with visible, luxurious wealth.

Perilous.kidExplanation: From a Latin word meaning 'a trial' — danger that tests you. The same Greek root 'peira' (to try) also gives us 'experience' and 'pirate'.

Precarious.kidExplanation: From Latin 'prex' (prayer). Something precarious is so uncertain you need to pray for it — it depends entirely on someone else's goodwill.

Turbulent.kidExplanation: From Latin 'turba' meaning a disorderly crowd. A turbulent crowd, sea, or time is full of churning, disordered movement.

Vigilant.rootMeaning: to keep watch, to stay awake
Vigilant.kidExplanation: From Latin 'vigil' — the soldier or monk who stays awake all night keeping watch. To be vigilant is to always be alert, never caught off guard.

Vivacious.kidExplanation: From Latin 'vivere' meaning to live. Something vivacious is buzzing with the energy of life — sparkling and full of vitality.

Obstinate.kidExplanation: Latin 'ob' (against) + a root meaning to fix firmly. An obstinate person has their mind fixed solidly in place — impossible to budge or persuade.

Zealous.kidExplanation: From Greek 'zelos' meaning fierce, passionate devotion. Shockingly, 'jealous' comes from the same root — both words are about burning intensity of feeling.

Abhorrent.kidNote: Big in Victorian novels and angry speeches. Less common today, but powerful when used.

Adamant.kidNote: Surprisingly still common today — politicians and headlines love it.

Audacious.kidNote: Almost faded out in the 1970s, then made a comeback — news writers love a "bold and audacious" plan.

Belligerent.kidNote: Peaked during the World Wars when nations were literally at war.

Benevolent.kidNote: Common in old novels for describing kind grandparents and rich donors.

Cacophony.kidNote: Used more now than ever — perfect for describing noisy traffic or messy music.

Calamity.kidNote: Victorian newspapers were full of calamities. Now mostly used dramatically.

Captivating.kidNote: Modern book reviews and adverts love this word.

Clandestine.kidNote: A favourite in spy novels and political reporting.

Compassion.kidNote: Used more today than at any point in history — a very modern, kind word.

Courageous.kidNote: Peaked during wartime but has stayed strong ever since.

Decrepit.kidNote: Common when Victorians wrote about crumbling old buildings. Rarer now.

Desolate.kidNote: Romantic poets and gothic novelists used it constantly.

Eerie.kidNote: Horror films and Halloween have kept this one alive and well.

Exquisite.kidNote: Has barely changed since Jane Austen's day — still the go-to word for beautiful things.

Ferocious.kidNote: Sports commentators and nature documentaries keep using it.

Forlorn.kidNote: A Romantic-era favourite (Keats wrote a famous line about it). Quite rare now.

Frantic.kidNote: Used a lot in news stories about busy people and emergencies.

Idyllic.kidNote: Travel adverts and Instagram captions love "idyllic" beaches.

Labyrinthine.kidNote: Was almost forgotten in the 1800s, now used for confusing bureaucracy.

Lethargic.kidNote: Health and fitness writers reach for this one a lot.

Magnanimous.kidNote: Was very common in 19th-century speeches about heroes and statesmen.

Majestic.kidNote: Nature writing and royal coverage keep it in heavy use.

Malevolent.kidNote: Fantasy novels and superhero films have made it more popular than ever.

Nefarious.kidNote: Dipped for a century, now bouncing back thanks to internet humour about "nefarious schemes".

Pristine.kidNote: More popular today than ever before — adverts love "pristine" beaches and condition.

Resilient.kidNote: Exploded in popularity in the 21st century — a very modern compliment.

Stealthy.kidNote: Video games and spy stories keep it in steady use.

Tempestuous.kidNote: A Romantic-era favourite for stormy seas and stormy feelings.

Tumultuous.kidNote: Common in 19th-century history books about revolutions and crowds.

Abundant.kidNote: More popular than ever — adverts, ecology reports, and school essays all use it.

Acrimonious.kidNote: A Victorian favourite for bitter arguments. Lawyers and journalists still reach for it.

Adversary.kidNote: Cold War spy fiction loved this word. Superhero comics and video games keep it alive.

Affable.kidNote: Victorian society novels used it constantly for charming gentlemen. Fading now.

Amiable.kidNote: Jane Austen used it often. Politely charming in old novels — less common today.

Anticipation.kidNote: Used more now than ever — marketing, psychology, and sports writing all love it.

Arduous.kidNote: Victorian explorers described arduous expeditions. Sports writers still use it.

Bewildered.kidNote: Charles Dickens loved this word for his confused characters. Still going strong.

Bleak.kidNote: Dickens wrote Bleak House. Post-war writers embraced it. Still widely used.

Bliss.kidNote: Wellness culture and social media have made 'bliss' more popular than ever.

Capricious.kidNote: Common in 19th-century novels for unpredictable weather and moody characters.

Chaotic.kidNote: Internet culture has made this word explode — 'absolute chaos' is everywhere.

Consensus.kidNote: Political reporting and business meetings have made this one of the most-used formal words.

Daunt.kidNote: Adventure stories loved this word. 'Nothing daunted him' was a classic heroic phrase.

Dazzling.kidNote: Fashion, sport, and entertainment journalism never tire of dazzling performances.

Defiant.kidNote: Protest culture and rebel heroes have driven this word back into heavy use.

Delicate.kidNote: One of the most reliable words in English — used for food, feelings, and negotiations.

Despondent.kidNote: Victorian literature loved brooding, despondent heroes. Less common in modern speech.

Dilapidated.kidNote: Gothic and Victorian fiction set stories in dilapidated mansions. Property writers still use it.

Dwindle.kidNote: Science writers use it for dwindling populations and resources. Quietly steady.

Elated.kidNote: Sports commentators and psychology writers love this word for joy and triumph.

Ephemeral.kidNote: Social media made this word fashionable — disappearing content is perfectly ephemeral.

Equilibrium.kidNote: Science, economics, and self-help books all use it. Finding equilibrium is very on-trend.

Eradicate.kidNote: Public health campaigns love this word — 'eradicate disease' sounds powerfully decisive.

Espionage.kidNote: Cold War spy thrillers made it famous. James Bond films have kept it in the public ear.

Exacerbate.kidNote: News reporting loves this word. 'Climate change exacerbates flooding' is a very modern sentence.

Flourish.kidNote: Gardening, business, and wellbeing writing all reach for 'flourish' to mean real growth.

Fragile.kidNote: Psychology, economics, and geopolitics all use 'fragile' — more popular than ever.

Gargantuan.kidNote: Food writing and sports commentary love gargantuan feats and portions.

Garrulous.kidNote: Victorian novelists used it for characters who talked too much. Quite rare now.

Hierarchy.kidNote: Business and social media have made this a go-to word — everyone discusses hierarchies now.

Indignant.kidNote: Victorian newspaper readers were frequently indignant. A little formal for everyday use now.

Ingenious.kidNote: Always popular for clever inventors and solutions. Technology writers use it constantly.

Jovial.kidNote: Dickens wrote many jovial characters. Still used for Father Christmas types but fading.

Jubilant.kidNote: Post-war victory headlines loved 'jubilant crowds'. Sports journalists still reach for it.

Melancholy.kidNote: The Romantic poets wrote endlessly of melancholy. Still used in music and poetry.

Menacing.kidNote: Crime reporting and film reviews love this word for threatening villains and weather.

Mercurial.kidNote: Sports journalists use it for brilliant, unpredictable players. A footballer's favourite word.

Mischievous.kidNote: Children's books have kept 'mischievous' in use for centuries. A timeless favourite.

Nostalgic.kidNote: One of the fastest-growing words — social media and retro culture have made nostalgia huge.

Notorious.kidNote: True crime podcasts and rap music have kept notorious in everyday use.

Ominous.kidNote: Weather forecasts, news headlines, and horror films all love 'ominous'. Steadily growing.

Opulent.kidNote: Interior design, fashion, and travel writing use 'opulent' constantly. Luxury brands love it.

Perilous.kidNote: Adventure stories from Treasure Island to fantasy epics have kept this one alive.

Precarious.kidNote: Economics and politics have latched onto 'precarious' — especially for insecure jobs and housing.

Turbulent.kidNote: From airline turbulence to turbulent times, this word crops up in news every week.

Vigilant.kidNote: Security, public health, and safety campaigns always urge you to stay vigilant.

Vivacious.kidNote: Victorian society columns loved a vivacious heroine. Charm has moved to other words now.

Obstinate.kidNote: Victorian novels were full of obstinate fathers and stubborn donkeys. Fading slowly.

Zealous.kidNote: Religious and political writing loved 'zealous' reformers. Still appears in formal writing.
